Because both of these are static, any changes made to the class of the `data` property within it will be made across all instances. If you are looking for the context to be instance-specific, you need to create a new instance of the class and assign it as a attribute during initialization.

```python
class B:
    def __init__(self):
        self.x = 0

class A:
    def __init__(self):
        self.b = B()
```

```python
>>> a = A()
>>> b = A()
>>> print(a.b.x)
0
>>> print(b.b.x)
0
>>> a.b.x = 1
>>> b.b.x = 2
>>> print(a.b.x)
1
>>> print(b.b.x)
2
```

Note that if you made the `x` attribute static as well, you would face a similar issue within the nested class.

If you do this, it'd be easier to use your implementation with Celery (or channels) to send these notifications without having to copy paste these lines. 😃

So if in the overlay template you don't want to use the ``javascript`` part and remove that block/rewrite it you will lose as well the tracking code (which is not desired)

Like this, we can separate the tracking code (see https://github.com/jmartinm/inspire-next/blob/6337772406d9aa40134d4697ccc88a26899bc465/inspire/base/templates/page.html#L50) and don't conflict with ``javascript`` block.

The permissions are actually affecting the `<prefix>_item` endpoint and not the `<prefix>_list` (aka "search") endpoint, so this protects `/records/1234` from someone that doesn't have permissions to access it but it doesn't protect `/records` from returning results containing record `1234`.

In order to filter on the level of the search results (e.g. for deposit search results), one has to do the filtering via  [customizing the `RecordsSearch` class that is used](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-deposit/blob/f243ea1d01ab0a3bc92ade3262d1abdd2bc32447/invenio_deposit/search.py#L36-L68) and passing it in in the [`search_class` field of the endpoint's config](https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-deposit/blob/f243ea1d01ab0a3bc92ade3262d1abdd2bc32447/invenio_deposit/config.py#L70)

In cases where the `Client` is returning us some sort of `Resource` it can set a reference to itself on `foo._client` before returning it.

In cases where we instantiate a `Resource` ourselves we're probably trying to create and save a new thing, no? This may be a not-great idea, but is it super-disruptive to require the user to supply `Client` in order to save that `Resource`? [It looks like `Resource.create` actually already takes a `client` arg](https://github.com/gadventures/gapipy/blob/6d19616cf14d435a76067a94879a69ea63ef4a6a/gapipy/resources/base.py#L34) and I guess `save`, `_create`, and `_update`, would have to change to receive a `client` (but could stash it on `self._client` for later use)

I'm not so keen on the name of this parameter.  It sounds like it should take a boolean.  But you would never give it a boolean, since `False` is already the default.  Writing `achromatic = 800` seems confusing to me.  As does `achromatic = bp['Z087']`, although that's possible somewhat better.

I think a better name would be `wavelength`, and then just indicate that a bandpass can be given, in which case the effective wavelength of that bandpass will be used.  `None` would mean to return a chromatic PSF.  That seems significantly less confusing to me.

Hmmmm.  If someone has some data that are periodic in nature, they might naturally just put together a single period without doing the edge duplication.  That actually feels pretty intuitive to me - i.e., use of `edge_mode='wrap'` means that you've gotten one period of an infinitely periodic function - more than saying you have to duplicate the first/last row and column.

@cburgdorf so after making the persistent chain, I'm not sure if it makes the most sense, as it requires the overhead of holding onto the chains through multiple benchmarks in memory, and the difference in benchmark execution time is very very small, (I can get an exact measurement if needed). 

I also don't think that the persistent chain really helps with isolation, because wthe initial setup transactions do not get included in the benchmarked data at all.  The setup transactions are sent and mined before any of the timing, and benchmarking even begins, and is not included in any of the benchmarked data or any of the totals. 

I also think that it makes things a bit harder to understand, but I think that is more my fault then anything, but still something that needs to be addressed.

side note: when I was testing initially, before realized to revert the canonical head of the chain, I found that if I ran the full transfer benchmark twice, the second time it would run more then a second slower in every VM version, because the head wouldn't reset, and the chain would start mining on block 201. ```python
```python
        from azure.mgmt.resource import SubscriptionClient

        subscriptions = self.load_cached_subscriptions()
        client_infos = []
        # TODO: eliminate redundant clients from the same tenant
        for s in subscriptions:
            client_infos.append((SubscriptionClient(self.get_login_credentials()[0]),s))

        refreshed_subs = []
        for c in client_infos:
            result = list(c[0].subscriptions.list())
            for r in result:
                setattr(r, 'tenant_id', c[1][_TENANT_ID])
            refreshed_subs += Profile._normalize_properties(c[1][_USER_ENTITY][_USER_NAME],
                                                            result,
                                                            c[1][_USER_ENTITY][_USER_TYPE]==_SERVICE_PRINCIPAL)

        # TODO: 
        # a: extract out following common code with "find_subscriptions_on_login", 
        # b. handle 'tenant only accounts' (no subscriptions)
        # c: ensure we maintain the active subscription
        self._set_subscriptions(refreshed_subs)
        return deepcopy(refreshed_subs)
```

Rather than searching for the specific strings `View on Afreeca` or `View on Twitch.tv` you might be better searching for any URL that is directly before the string `View on`. That way you can find other embedded streams, I noticed that there are some goodgame.ru streams too. You could use a regex like:
```python
stream_address_re = re.compile(r'''href\s*=\s*"([^"]*?)"[^<>]+?>\s*View\s+on\s+''', re.IGNORECASE | re.MULTILINE)
```
Then extract the matched URL (if any), and pass it to `self.session.streams`;
```python
stream_url_match = stream_address_re.search(res.text)
if stream_url_match:
    stream_url = stream_url_match.group(1)
    self.logger.info("Attempting to play streams from {0}", stream_url)
    return self.session.streams(stream_url)
```

That way you will be able to support any embedded streams that streamlink supports, not just twitch/afreeca.
